Mathematics.teks_description: Graph and write the inverse of a function using notation such as f^-1(x)
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers advanced functions for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS 2A.2(B)). Students should graph and write inverses of functions. Finding inverses: swap x and y, solve for y; f^(-1)(x) notation. Graphical relationship: inverse is reflection over y = x. Conditions: only one-to-one functions have inverses (pass horizontal line test); restrict domain to make non-one-to-one functions invertible. Composition verification: f(f^(-1)(x)) = x and f^(-1)(f(x)) = x. Advanced transformations: combining multiple transformations (order matters), transformations of absolute value, rational, exponential, and logarithmic functions. Piecewise functions: defined by different rules on different intervals; continuity and differentiability at boundary points. Vocabulary: inverse function, one-to-one, horizontal line test, composition, domain restriction, piecewise function, transformation, reflection, parent function.

Mathematics.skill: Polynomial functions (division, remainder theorem, factor theorem)
Mathematics.teks_description: Determine quotients of polynomials divided by linear or quadratic polynomials
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers polynomial division for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS 2A.7(C)). Students should find quotients of polynomials divided by linear or quadratic divisors. Long division: systematic process for dividing any polynomial by any polynomial of equal or lesser degree. Synthetic division: efficient shortcut when dividing by (x - c). Remainder theorem: when polynomial f(x) is divided by (x - c), the remainder equals f(c). Factor theorem: (x - c) is a factor of f(x) if and only if f(c) = 0. Finding zeros: use rational root theorem (possible rational zeros = ±factors of constant / factors of leading coefficient), test candidates using synthetic division, then factor the reduced polynomial. Polynomial inequalities: find zeros, test intervals, determine sign. Key concepts: Fundamental Theorem of Algebra, relationship between factors and zeros, writing polynomials from given zeros, Descartes' Rule of Signs. Vocabulary: polynomial division, quotient, remainder, synthetic division, Remainder Theorem, Factor Theorem, rational root theorem, zero, factor, Descartes' Rule of Signs.

Mathematics.teks_description: Determine asymptotic restrictions and represent domain/range using notation and set theory
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers advanced rational function analysis for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS 2A.6(K)). Students should perform complete analysis including asymptotic behavior, domain, and range. Advanced topics: oblique (slant) asymptotes when numerator degree exceeds denominator degree by 1 (found by polynomial division), holes vs. vertical asymptotes (factor and simplify), end behavior analysis. Graphing strategy: (1) factor and simplify, (2) find domain restrictions, (3) identify vertical asymptotes and holes, (4) determine horizontal or oblique asymptote, (5) find intercepts, (6) determine sign behavior in each interval. Solving rational equations: find LCD, multiply through, solve, check for extraneous solutions (values that make original denominator zero). Rational inequalities: find critical values (zeros and restrictions), test intervals, include endpoints where ≤ or ≥ (but never include restrictions). Applications: inverse variation, average rate problems, mixture problems. Vocabulary: rational function, vertical asymptote, horizontal asymptote, oblique asymptote, hole, domain, range, end behavior, extraneous solution, interval notation.

Mathematics.teks_description: Generate and solve logarithmic equations in mathematical and real-world problems
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers exponential and logarithmic applications for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.5(H)). Students should generate and solve logarithmic equations in real-world contexts. Applications of exponential functions: population growth P(t) = P0e^(rt), radioactive decay N(t) = N0e^(-λt), compound interest A = P(1+r/n)^(nt), continuous compounding A = Pe^(rt), Newton's law of cooling T(t) = Ts + (T0-Ts)e^(-kt). Applications of logarithmic functions: pH = -log[H+], Richter scale M = log(I/I0), decibel scale dB = 10log(I/I0), doubling time t = ln(2)/r, half-life t = ln(2)/λ. Solving techniques: isolate exponential/log expression, apply inverse operation (log or exponentiation), use properties of logarithms to simplify. Key concepts: logarithmic scales compress large ranges, exponential models describe many natural phenomena, choosing between exponential and linear models based on data. Vocabulary: exponential growth, exponential decay, half-life, doubling time, compound interest, continuous compounding, logarithmic scale, pH, decibel, Newton's law of cooling.

Mathematics.skill: Trigonometric functions (law of sines, law of cosines)
Mathematics.teks_description: Use the Law of Sines in mathematical and real-world problems
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers the law of sines and law of cosines for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.4(G)). Students should solve oblique (non-right) triangles. Law of Sines: a/sin(A) = b/sin(B) = c/sin(C); used when given AAS, ASA, or SSA (ambiguous case). Law of Cosines: c² = a² + b² - 2ab·cos(C); used when given SAS or SSS. Ambiguous case (SSA): zero, one, or two triangles may be possible; compare the given side with the altitude to determine. Area formulas: A = ½ab·sin(C) using two sides and included angle; Heron's formula A = √(s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)) where s = (a+b+c)/2. Applications: navigation, surveying, force triangles, distance problems with non-right angles. Problem-solving strategy: determine what information is given, choose appropriate law, solve systematically. Vocabulary: oblique triangle, Law of Sines, Law of Cosines, ambiguous case, included angle, Heron's formula, bearing, angle of elevation, angle of depression.

Mathematics.teks_description: Use trigonometric identities including reciprocal, quotient, Pythagorean, sum and difference identities
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers advanced trigonometric identities for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.5(M)). Students should apply sum, difference, double-angle, and half-angle identities. Sum and difference: sin(A±B) = sinA·cosB ± cosA·sinB, cos(A±B) = cosA·cosB ∓ sinA·sinB, tan(A±B) = (tanA ± tanB)/(1 ∓ tanA·tanB). Double-angle: sin(2A) = 2sinA·cosA, cos(2A) = cos²A - sin²A = 2cos²A - 1 = 1 - 2sin²A, tan(2A) = 2tanA/(1 - tan²A). Half-angle: sin(A/2) = ±√((1-cosA)/2), cos(A/2) = ±√((1+cosA)/2). Applications: simplifying expressions, solving equations, evaluating exact values of non-standard angles (e.g., cos(15°) using cos(45°-30°)). Product-to-sum and sum-to-product formulas for advanced applications. Vocabulary: sum identity, difference identity, double-angle identity, half-angle identity, product-to-sum, verify, simplify, exact value.

Mathematics.teks_description: Represent the addition of vectors and multiplication of a vector by a scalar geometrically and symbolically
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers vector operations for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.4(J)). Students should represent vector addition and scalar multiplication geometrically and symbolically. Vector operations: addition v + w = <v1+w1, v2+w2>, subtraction, scalar multiplication cv = <cv1, cv2>. Magnitude: |v| = √(v1² + v2²). Unit vector: v/|v|. Dot product: v · w = v1w1 + v2w2 = |v||w|cos θ; scalar result; used to find angle between vectors, determine orthogonality (dot product = 0), find projections. Vector projection: proj_w(v) = ((v·w)/(w·w))w. Cross product (3D): v × w = <v2w3-v3w2, v3w1-v1w3, v1w2-v2w1>; vector result perpendicular to both inputs; magnitude |v||w|sin θ = area of parallelogram. Applications: work (W = F·d), torque (τ = r × F), navigation, physics problems. Vocabulary: vector, scalar, magnitude, direction, component, unit vector, dot product, cross product, projection, orthogonal, parallel, resultant.

Mathematics.teks_description: Solve systems using Gaussian elimination, matrices, or substitution
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers matrix operations for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS 2A.3(B)). Students should use matrices to solve systems and understand matrix algebra. Operations: addition/subtraction (same dimensions), scalar multiplication, matrix multiplication (rows by columns). Determinant: 2×2 det = ad-bc; 3×3 by cofactor expansion; det = 0 means matrix is singular (no inverse). Inverse: A^(-1) exists when det(A) ≠ 0; for 2×2: A^(-1) = (1/det)[d,-b;-c,a]; solving systems AX = B gives X = A^(-1)B. Gaussian elimination: augment [A|I], row reduce to [I|A^(-1)]. Properties: (AB)^(-1) = B^(-1)A^(-1), det(AB) = det(A)det(B). Cramer's rule: solve systems using determinants. Key concepts: matrix representation of linear transformations, rotation and reflection matrices. Vocabulary: matrix, determinant, inverse, singular, nonsingular, augmented matrix, row echelon form, Gaussian elimination, Cramer's rule, identity matrix, cofactor, adjugate.

Mathematics.teks_description: Formulate systems of equations including three linear equations in three variables
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers systems of equations using matrices for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS 2A.3(A)). Students should formulate and solve systems of three linear equations in three unknowns. Representing systems: coefficient matrix, variable matrix, constant matrix; AX = B. Solving methods: substitution (solve one equation for one variable, substitute into others), elimination (add/subtract equations to eliminate variables), Gaussian elimination (row reduce augmented matrix to echelon form), matrix inverse method (X = A^(-1)B), Cramer's rule (using determinants). Solution types: unique solution (planes intersect at a point), no solution (inconsistent — parallel planes), infinitely many solutions (dependent — planes share a line or are identical). Applications: mixture problems, cost problems, network flow, balancing chemical equations, fitting curves through points. Key concepts: geometric interpretation in 3D (each equation represents a plane), identifying solution type from row-reduced matrix, parametric solutions for dependent systems. Vocabulary: system of equations, three variables, augmented matrix, echelon form, Gaussian elimination, consistent, inconsistent, dependent, independent, parameter, plane, intersection.

Mathematics.teks_description: Use the characteristics of a hyperbola to write the equation of a hyperbola with center (h,k)
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers advanced conic sections for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.3(I)). Students should write and analyze equations of hyperbolas and all conics with center (h,k). Hyperbola: (x-h)²/a² - (y-k)²/b² = 1 (horizontal transverse axis) or (y-k)²/a² - (x-h)²/b² = 1 (vertical). Features: center (h,k), vertices (±a from center along transverse axis), foci (±c from center, c² = a² + b²), asymptotes y-k = ±(b/a)(x-h) or ±(a/b)(x-h). General second-degree equation: Ax² + Bxy + Cy² + Dx + Ey + F = 0; discriminant B²-4AC determines type (negative = ellipse/circle, zero = parabola, positive = hyperbola). Converting general to standard form: complete the square for x and y terms. Rotation of conics: when B ≠ 0, axes are rotated; rotation angle θ = ½ arctan(B/(A-C)). Eccentricity: e = c/a; circle (e=0), ellipse (0<e<1), parabola (e=1), hyperbola (e>1). Vocabulary: hyperbola, transverse axis, conjugate axis, asymptote, eccentricity, focus, vertex, general form, standard form, rotation of axes, discriminant, degenerate conic.

Mathematics.teks_description: Calculate the nth term and partial sum of geometric series and sum of infinite geometric series when it exists
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers convergence and divergence for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.5(E)). Students should analyze geometric series for convergence. Geometric series: Σ(ar^n); finite sum Sn = a(1-r^n)/(1-r); infinite sum S = a/(1-r) only when |r| < 1 (convergent). When |r| ≥ 1, the series diverges (sum grows without bound or oscillates). nth term: an = a1 × r^(n-1). Partial sums: sum of first n terms; sequence of partial sums determines convergence. Key concepts: convergence means partial sums approach a finite limit, divergence means they do not; geometric series is the foundational model for convergence analysis. Applications: repeating decimals as infinite geometric series (0.333... = 1/3), present value of annuities, drug dosing models, fractal geometry (Koch snowflake perimeter), bouncing ball total distance. Telescoping series: consecutive terms cancel, leaving finite sum. Vocabulary: sequence, series, partial sum, convergent, divergent, geometric series, common ratio, infinite series, limit, sum to infinity, telescoping series.

Mathematics.teks_description: Determine various types of discontinuities in the interval as they relate to functions
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers introduction to limits for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.2(L)). Students should understand various types of discontinuities and the concept of limits. Limit definition: the value f(x) approaches as x approaches a value c; written lim(x→c) f(x) = L. Types of discontinuities: removable (hole — limit exists but function value differs or is undefined), jump (left and right limits exist but differ), infinite (function approaches ±∞, vertical asymptote). One-sided limits: lim(x→c⁻) and lim(x→c⁺); two-sided limit exists only when both one-sided limits exist and are equal. Evaluating limits: direct substitution (when function is continuous), factoring and simplifying (for 0/0 forms), rationalizing (multiply by conjugate), graphical and numerical (table of values). Limits at infinity: horizontal asymptotes; for rational functions, compare degrees of numerator and denominator. Key concepts: limits describe behavior near a point (not necessarily at it), continuity requires f(c) = lim(x→c) f(x), indeterminate forms (0/0, ∞/∞). Vocabulary: limit, continuity, discontinuity, removable, jump, infinite, one-sided limit, indeterminate form, horizontal asymptote, behavior, approach.

Mathematics.teks_description: Analyze and describe end behavior of functions using infinity notation
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers introduction to derivatives for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.2(J)). Students should analyze end behavior of functions and understand the derivative as a rate of change. Derivative concept: instantaneous rate of change; slope of tangent line at a point; f'(x) = lim(h→0) [f(x+h) - f(x)]/h. Basic rules: power rule d/dx(x^n) = nx^(n-1), constant rule d/dx(c) = 0, constant multiple rule d/dx(cf) = cf', sum/difference rule d/dx(f±g) = f'±g'. Product rule: d/dx(fg) = f'g + fg'. Quotient rule: d/dx(f/g) = (f'g - fg')/g². Chain rule: d/dx(f(g(x))) = f'(g(x))·g'(x). End behavior: analyze lim(x→±∞) to determine horizontal asymptotes and long-term trends. Key concepts: derivative as slope function, relationship between function and its derivative, where derivative equals zero (critical points), positive derivative means increasing function, negative means decreasing. Vocabulary: derivative, instantaneous rate of change, tangent line, slope, power rule, product rule, quotient rule, chain rule, critical point, increasing, decreasing.

Mathematics.skill: Applications of derivatives (rates of change, optimization)
Mathematics.teks_description: Determine and analyze key features of functions including domain, range, zeros, relative max/min
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers derivative applications for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.2(I)). Students should determine key features of functions including domain, range, zeros, and extrema. First derivative test: find critical points (f'(x) = 0 or undefined), test sign changes to identify local maxima and minima. Second derivative test: f''(x) > 0 at critical point means local min, f''(x) < 0 means local max. Optimization: identify quantity to maximize/minimize, express as function of one variable, find critical points, verify maximum/minimum, answer in context. Related rates: identify changing quantities, write equation relating them, differentiate with respect to time, substitute known values, solve. Applications: velocity and acceleration (v = ds/dt, a = dv/dt), marginal cost/revenue, population growth rate, optimization of area, volume, profit, cost. Key concepts: concavity (f'' > 0 concave up, f'' < 0 concave down), inflection points, interpreting derivative graphs. Vocabulary: optimization, critical point, local maximum, local minimum, absolute maximum, absolute minimum, related rates, concavity, inflection point, marginal, velocity, acceleration.

Mathematics.teks_description: Evaluate finite sums and geometric series written in sigma notation
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers introduction to integrals for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.5(A)). Students should evaluate finite sums and develop understanding of integration as accumulation. Sigma notation: Σ represents summation; Σ(i=1 to n) f(i) = f(1) + f(2) + ... + f(n). Summation formulas: Σi = n(n+1)/2, Σi² = n(n+1)(2n+1)/6, Σi³ = [n(n+1)/2]². Riemann sums: approximate area under curve by dividing into rectangles (left, right, midpoint); more rectangles = better approximation. Definite integral: ∫[a,b] f(x)dx = lim(n→∞) Σf(xi)Δx; represents exact area under curve. Fundamental Theorem of Calculus: ∫[a,b] f(x)dx = F(b) - F(a) where F'(x) = f(x). Basic antiderivatives: ∫x^n dx = x^(n+1)/(n+1) + C (n≠-1), ∫1/x dx = ln|x| + C, ∫e^x dx = e^x + C. Applications: area between curves, displacement from velocity, total accumulation. Vocabulary: integral, antiderivative, Riemann sum, sigma notation, area under curve, definite integral, indefinite integral, Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, accumulation, constant of integration.

Mathematics.teks_description: Explain how sample statistics and confidence levels combine to construct confidence intervals
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers advanced probability and statistics for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS S.6(A)). Students should understand confidence intervals and their construction. Confidence intervals: range of values likely containing the population parameter; CI = point estimate ± margin of error. For population mean (σ known): x̄ ± z*(σ/√n). Common confidence levels: 90% (z* = 1.645), 95% (z* = 1.96), 99% (z* = 2.576). Components: sample statistic (point estimate), critical value (z* or t*), standard error (σ/√n or s/√n). Increasing confidence level widens the interval; increasing sample size narrows it. Interpretation: we are C% confident that the true population parameter falls within the interval. t-distribution: used when σ is unknown and n is small; approaches normal as n increases. Hypothesis testing introduction: null and alternative hypotheses, p-values, Type I and Type II errors. Vocabulary: confidence interval, margin of error, critical value, standard error, sample size, confidence level, point estimate, t-distribution, hypothesis test, p-value, null hypothesis, significance level.

Mathematics.teks_description: Examine scatterplots to identify patterns, linearity, outliers, and influential points
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers data analysis and regression for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS S.7(A)). Students should examine scatterplots to identify patterns, linearity, outliers, and influential points. Scatterplot analysis: identify direction (positive, negative, none), form (linear, quadratic, exponential), strength (strong, moderate, weak), unusual features (outliers, clusters, gaps). Linear regression: least-squares regression line ŷ = a + bx minimizes sum of squared residuals; b = r(sy/sx), a = ȳ - bx̄. Residual analysis: residual = observed - predicted; residual plot should show random scatter for good fit; patterns in residuals suggest model inadequacy. Correlation coefficient r: measures linear association strength and direction; r² represents proportion of variance explained. Influential points: high leverage points (extreme x-values) and outliers can dramatically affect regression line. Multiple regression introduction: ŷ = a + b1x1 + b2x2 for multiple predictors. Transformations: linearizing curved data using log or power transformations. Vocabulary: scatterplot, regression, residual, correlation, outlier, influential point, leverage, least squares, coefficient of determination, transformation, linearity, residual plot.

Mathematics.skill: Complex numbers (polar form, De Moivre's theorem)
Mathematics.teks_description: Graph exponential, logarithmic, rational, polynomial, power, trigonometric, and piecewise functions
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers complex numbers in polar form for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.2(F)). Students should represent complex numbers in multiple forms and graph them. Rectangular form: z = a + bi. Polar (trigonometric) form: z = r(cos θ + i sin θ) = r·cis θ, where r = |z| = √(a²+b²) is the modulus and θ = arg(z) = tan^(-1)(b/a) is the argument. Euler's form: z = re^(iθ). Converting: a = r cos θ, b = r sin θ. Multiplication in polar form: z1·z2 = r1r2 · cis(θ1+θ2) — multiply moduli, add arguments. Division: z1/z2 = (r1/r2) · cis(θ1-θ2). De Moivre's theorem: z^n = r^n · cis(nθ); powerful for computing powers. nth roots: z^(1/n) = r^(1/n) · cis((θ+2πk)/n) for k = 0,1,...,n-1; produces n equally-spaced roots on a circle. Applications: roots of unity, signal processing, electrical engineering (impedance). Vocabulary: complex number, modulus, argument, polar form, rectangular form, De Moivre's theorem, roots of unity, Euler's formula, complex plane, Argand diagram.

Mathematics.teks_description: Graph a set of parametric equations
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers parametric equations for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.3(A)). Students should graph sets of parametric equations and understand their applications. Parametric equations: define x and y separately as functions of a parameter t: x = f(t), y = g(t). Graphing: create table of t, x, y values; plot (x,y) points; indicate direction with arrows as t increases. Eliminating the parameter: solve one equation for t, substitute into the other to get Cartesian equation. Common parametric curves: line (x = x0+at, y = y0+bt), circle (x = r cos t, y = r sin t), ellipse (x = a cos t, y = b sin t), parabola. Applications: projectile motion (x = v0 cos θ · t, y = v0 sin θ · t - ½gt²), cycloids, planetary orbits, animation paths. Derivatives: dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt), giving slope of tangent to parametric curve. Key concepts: same Cartesian curve can have multiple parametric representations, parameter often represents time, direction of traversal matters. Vocabulary: parametric equation, parameter, Cartesian equation, eliminate the parameter, direction of traversal, cycloid, projectile, curve, arc length.

Mathematics.teks_description: Graph points in the polar coordinate system and convert between rectangular and polar coordinates
Mathematics.prompt: This topic covers polar coordinates for 11th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.3(D)). Students should graph points and convert between rectangular and polar coordinate systems. Polar coordinates: point represented as (r, θ) where r is distance from origin (pole) and θ is angle from positive x-axis (polar axis). Conversions: x = r cos θ, y = r sin θ (polar to rectangular); r = √(x²+y²), θ = tan^(-1)(y/x) (rectangular to polar). Multiple representations: same point can be (r, θ), (r, θ+2π), (-r, θ+π). Common polar curves: circles (r = a, r = a cos θ, r = a sin θ), cardioids (r = a + a cos θ), limacons (r = a + b cos θ), roses (r = a cos(nθ) — n petals if n odd, 2n if even), spirals (r = aθ), lemniscates (r² = a² cos(2θ)). Graphing: use table of θ and r values, identify symmetry (about polar axis, about θ = π/2, about pole). Key concepts: converting equations between coordinate systems, identifying curve types, finding intersections of polar curves (set equations equal and solve, also check r = 0). Vocabulary: polar coordinates, pole, polar axis, rectangular coordinates, cardioid, limacon, rose curve, lemniscate, spiral, symmetry, conversion.

Science.teks_description: Analyze and critique scientific explanations using empirical evidence in biology
Science.prompt: This topic covers advanced biology for 11th grade Science students (TEKS B.4(A)). Students apply rigorous scientific analysis to evaluate biological claims and research. At this level, students engage with primary scientific literature, evaluate experimental methodology, analyze data with statistical reasoning, and distinguish between correlation and causation. Key concepts: research design (randomized controlled trials, cohort studies, case-control studies), statistical analysis (p-values, confidence intervals, effect sizes), peer review process, evaluating sources for bias and validity. Students should understand how scientific consensus forms, the self-correcting nature of science, and current ethical debates in biology (gain-of-function research, human genetic modification, animal testing). Applications: reading and interpreting scientific graphs and data tables, designing experiments with proper controls, writing evidence-based scientific arguments. Vocabulary: empirical evidence, peer review, statistical significance, p-value, correlation, causation, bias, control, variable, replication, validity, reliability, scientific consensus.

Science.teks_description: Describe gene expression and protein synthesis using models
Science.prompt: This topic covers molecular genetics for 11th grade Science students (TEKS B.7(B)). Students should understand gene expression and regulation at the molecular level. Gene expression: the process by which DNA information is used to produce functional proteins (central dogma: DNA → mRNA → protein). Regulation levels: transcriptional (most common — promoters, enhancers, silencers, transcription factors), post-transcriptional (mRNA processing: capping, polyadenylation, splicing; microRNA regulation), translational (controlling ribosome activity), post-translational (protein folding, modification, degradation). Epigenetics: heritable changes in gene expression without DNA sequence changes; mechanisms include DNA methylation and histone modification. Operons (prokaryotic regulation): lac operon (inducible, activates with lactose) and trp operon (repressible, deactivates with tryptophan). Genomics: study of entire genomes; Human Genome Project revealed ~20,000 protein-coding genes; comparative genomics reveals evolutionary relationships. Vocabulary: gene expression, transcription factor, promoter, enhancer, operon, epigenetics, methylation, histone, gene regulation, genomics, proteomics, central dogma, mRNA processing, exon, intron.

Science.teks_description: Discuss PCR, gel electrophoresis, and genetic engineering applications and implications
Science.prompt: This topic covers advanced biotechnology for 11th grade Science students (TEKS B.7(D)). Students should discuss cutting-edge biotechnology applications and their implications. Cloning: reproductive cloning (producing genetically identical organism, e.g., Dolly the sheep via somatic cell nuclear transfer) and therapeutic cloning (producing embryonic stem cells for medical use). Gene therapy: replacing defective genes with functional copies; viral vectors deliver corrected genes to target cells; challenges include immune response, targeting accuracy, and insertional mutagenesis. CRISPR-Cas9: revolutionary gene editing tool using guide RNA to direct Cas9 enzyme to specific DNA locations for precise cuts; applications in treating genetic diseases, creating disease-resistant crops, combating invasive species (gene drives). GMOs: organisms with modified DNA; Bt crops (insect resistance), herbicide-tolerant crops, golden rice (vitamin A enriched), AquAdvantage salmon (faster growth). Ethical debates: germline vs. somatic editing, designer babies, environmental release of modified organisms, access equity, informed consent, patenting life forms. Vocabulary: cloning, somatic cell nuclear transfer, gene therapy, CRISPR-Cas9, guide RNA, gene drive, GMO, transgenic, vector, stem cell, bioethics, germline editing, informed consent.

Science.teks_description: Analyze genetic drift, gene flow, mutation, and recombination effects on gene pools
Science.prompt: This topic covers evolutionary biology for 11th grade Science students (TEKS B.10(D)). Students should analyze how genetic drift, gene flow, mutation, and recombination affect gene pools. Population genetics: study of allele frequency changes in populations. Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium: p² + 2pq + q² = 1 and p + q = 1; conditions for equilibrium (no mutation, random mating, no selection, large population, no migration). Evolutionary forces: genetic drift (random changes; bottleneck effect after population crash; founder effect in new colonies), gene flow (migration of alleles between populations; homogenizes populations), mutation (creates new alleles; source of all variation), natural selection (differential reproduction; directional, stabilizing, disruptive, sexual selection), recombination (crossing over and independent assortment create new allele combinations). Phylogenetics: constructing evolutionary trees from molecular data (DNA sequences, protein comparisons); molecular clocks estimate divergence times. Microevolution vs. macroevolution. Vocabulary: allele frequency, gene pool, Hardy-Weinberg, genetic drift, bottleneck effect, founder effect, gene flow, mutation, recombination, selection, phylogeny, molecular clock, clade, speciation.

Science.teks_description: Explain how environmental changes affect biodiversity and ecosystem stability
Science.prompt: This topic covers conservation biology and global change for 11th grade Science students (TEKS B.13(D)). Students should understand how environmental changes affect biodiversity and ecosystem stability at a sophisticated level. Biodiversity threats (HIPPCO): Habitat destruction, Invasive species, Population growth, Pollution, Climate change, Overexploitation. Conservation strategies: in-situ (protected areas, wildlife corridors, habitat restoration) and ex-situ (zoos, seed banks, captive breeding). Minimum viable population: smallest population that can survive long term; small populations face inbreeding depression, genetic drift, and demographic stochasticity. Landscape ecology: habitat fragmentation creates edge effects (altered microclimate, increased predation at edges), reduces interior habitat, and disrupts wildlife movement; corridors connect fragments. Global change: climate change effects on species distributions, phenology (timing of biological events), coral bleaching, sea-level rise, ocean acidification, tipping points in ecosystems. Ecosystem services valuation: assigning economic value to ecosystem functions supports conservation arguments. Vocabulary: conservation biology, biodiversity, HIPPCO, habitat fragmentation, corridor, edge effect, minimum viable population, in-situ, ex-situ, ecosystem services, phenology, tipping point.

Science.teks_description: Analyze animal system interactions for regulation including homeostasis and feedback mechanisms
Science.prompt: This topic covers human physiology for 11th grade Science students (TEKS B.12(A)). Students should analyze animal system interactions for homeostasis and feedback mechanisms at an advanced level. Negative feedback: response opposes stimulus to maintain set point; examples: thermoregulation (hypothalamus detects temperature change → vasodilation/vasoconstriction, sweating/shivering), blood glucose regulation (insulin from beta cells lowers glucose, glucagon from alpha cells raises it), blood pressure regulation (baroreceptors → cardiac output and vessel diameter adjustments). Positive feedback: response amplifies stimulus; examples: childbirth (oxytocin increases contractions), blood clotting cascade, lactation. Endocrine system: hormones as chemical messengers; hypothalamic-pituitary axis controls many hormones; steroid hormones (cross membrane, affect gene expression) vs. peptide hormones (bind surface receptors, use second messengers like cAMP). Nervous-endocrine integration: hypothalamus links nervous and endocrine systems; fight-or-flight response (sympathetic nervous system + adrenal hormones). Key concepts: receptor-control center-effector model, antagonistic hormone pairs, homeostatic imbalance leads to disease. Vocabulary: homeostasis, negative feedback, positive feedback, set point, hypothalamus, pituitary, hormone, receptor, effector, sympathetic, parasympathetic, insulin, glucagon.

Science.teks_description: Analyze and critique scientific explanations using empirical evidence in chemistry
Science.prompt: This topic covers advanced chemistry concepts for 11th grade Science students (TEKS C.4(A)). At this level, students apply sophisticated analytical skills to evaluate chemical theories and experimental evidence. Students should: critically analyze the development of atomic models (Dalton → Thomson → Rutherford → Bohr → quantum mechanical model), understand how spectroscopic evidence supports atomic theory, evaluate competing theories and models in chemistry, and design rigorous experiments with appropriate controls. Advanced scientific reasoning: distinguishing between models and reality, understanding limitations of models (ideal gas model breaks down at high pressure and low temperature), assessing reliability and validity of experimental data, applying error analysis (systematic vs. random error, percent error calculations). Students should engage with current chemistry research topics including green chemistry, materials science, and computational chemistry. Vocabulary: model, theory, empirical evidence, spectroscopy, error analysis, systematic error, random error, percent error, precision, accuracy, reliability, validity, green chemistry.

Science.skill: Chemical bonding (VSEPR theory, molecular orbital theory)
Science.teks_description: Classify and draw electron dot structures for molecules with various geometries per VSEPR theory
Science.prompt: This topic covers advanced chemical bonding for 11th grade Science students (TEKS C.7(C)). Students should classify molecular geometries using VSEPR theory and draw electron dot structures. VSEPR (Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion) theory: electron groups around a central atom arrange to minimize repulsion. Molecular geometries: linear (2 bonding groups, 0 lone pairs, 180°), trigonal planar (3,0, 120°), bent (2,1 or 2,2), tetrahedral (4,0, 109.5°), trigonal pyramidal (3,1, ~107°), trigonal bipyramidal (5,0, 90°/120°), octahedral (6,0, 90°). Key concepts: lone pairs occupy more space than bonding pairs, multiple bonds count as one electron group, expanded octets possible for period 3+ elements. Lewis structures: draw for molecules with multiple bonds, resonance structures (delocalized electrons, e.g., ozone, benzene), exceptions to octet rule (incomplete octets for B, expanded octets for S, P). Polarity: polar bonds + molecular geometry determine molecular polarity; symmetrical molecules can be nonpolar despite polar bonds. Vocabulary: VSEPR, electron geometry, molecular geometry, lone pair, bonding pair, linear, trigonal planar, tetrahedral, bent, trigonal pyramidal, resonance, polarity, dipole moment.

Science.teks_description: Describe and calculate relationships among volume, pressure, moles, and temperature for ideal gases
Science.prompt: This topic covers gases for 11th grade Science students (TEKS C.10(B)). Students should apply gas law equations and understand kinetic molecular theory. KMT postulates: gas particles are in constant random motion, negligible volume compared to container, no intermolecular forces, elastic collisions, average KE proportional to absolute temperature. Gas laws: Boyle's (P1V1 = P2V2, constant T), Charles's (V1/T1 = V2/T2, constant P), Gay-Lussac's (P1/T1 = P2/T2, constant V), Avogadro's (V1/n1 = V2/n2, constant T,P), Combined (P1V1/T1 = P2V2/T2), Ideal (PV = nRT, R = 0.0821 L·atm/mol·K). Dalton's law of partial pressures: Ptotal = P1 + P2 + ... Graham's law of effusion: rate1/rate2 = √(M2/M1). STP: 0°C, 1 atm; molar volume = 22.4 L/mol. Real gases: deviate from ideal at high P and low T; van der Waals equation accounts for molecular volume and intermolecular forces. Key concepts: temperature must be in Kelvin, units must match, molar volume at STP. Vocabulary: kinetic molecular theory, ideal gas, real gas, pressure, volume, temperature, moles, Boyle's law, Charles's law, ideal gas law, Dalton's law, Graham's law, STP, partial pressure.

Science.teks_description: Distinguish among solution types including electrolytes, nonelectrolytes, and saturation levels
Science.prompt: This topic covers solutions and colligative properties for 11th grade Science students (TEKS C.11(B)). Students should distinguish among solution types and understand colligative properties. Solution types: electrolytes (conduct electricity when dissolved — strong electrolytes fully dissociate, weak electrolytes partially dissociate) vs. nonelectrolytes (do not conduct — molecular compounds like sugar). Saturation levels: unsaturated, saturated, supersaturated. Concentration units: molarity (M = mol/L), molality (m = mol/kg solvent), mole fraction (χ), percent by mass. Colligative properties: depend on number of solute particles, not identity. Boiling point elevation: ΔTb = iKbm. Freezing point depression: ΔTf = iKfm. Osmotic pressure: π = iMRT. Vapor pressure lowering: Raoult's law Psolution = χsolvent × P°solvent. Van't Hoff factor (i): accounts for dissociation of electrolytes (NaCl: i = 2, CaCl2: i = 3). Applications: antifreeze, salting roads, IV solutions, dialysis, desalination. Vocabulary: electrolyte, nonelectrolyte, colligative property, boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure, molality, van't Hoff factor, dissociation, Raoult's law.

Science.teks_description: Define acids and bases and distinguish between Arrhenius and Bronsted-Lowry definitions
Science.prompt: This topic covers acid-base equilibrium for 11th grade Science students (TEKS C.12(B)). Students should distinguish between Arrhenius and Bronsted-Lowry definitions and understand acid-base equilibria. Arrhenius: acids produce H+ in water, bases produce OH-. Bronsted-Lowry: acids donate protons, bases accept protons; conjugate acid-base pairs. Lewis definition: acids accept electron pairs, bases donate electron pairs. Ka and Kb: acid and base dissociation constants; larger Ka means stronger acid. Water autoionization: Kw = [H+][OH-] = 1.0 × 10^-14 at 25°C; pH + pOH = 14. Weak acid equilibrium: Ka = [H+][A-]/[HA]; calculate pH of weak acids using ICE tables. Buffers: solutions that resist pH changes; contain weak acid + conjugate base or weak base + conjugate acid; Henderson-Hasselbalch equation pH = pKa + log([A-]/[HA]). Buffer capacity: amount of acid or base a buffer can neutralize. Titration curves: strong acid-strong base (equivalence at pH 7), weak acid-strong base (equivalence > pH 7), half-equivalence point (pH = pKa). Vocabulary: Arrhenius, Bronsted-Lowry, Lewis, conjugate acid, conjugate base, Ka, Kb, Kw, buffer, Henderson-Hasselbalch, titration, equivalence point, indicator, amphiprotic.

Science.teks_description: Differentiate among acid-base, precipitation, and oxidation-reduction reactions
Science.prompt: This topic covers electrochemistry for 11th grade Science students (TEKS C.9(B)). Students should understand the connection between redox reactions and electrical energy. Galvanic (voltaic) cells: spontaneous redox reactions generate electricity. Components: anode (oxidation, negative), cathode (reduction, positive), salt bridge (maintains electrical neutrality), external circuit (electron flow). Cell notation: anode | anode solution || cathode solution | cathode. Standard cell potential: E°cell = E°cathode - E°anode; positive E°cell = spontaneous. Standard reduction potentials: table of E° values; more positive = stronger oxidizing agent. Electrolytic cells: use external electricity to drive nonspontaneous reactions; applications include electroplating, electrolysis of water, metal purification, production of aluminum. Faraday's laws: relate charge (q = It) to amount of substance produced/consumed; 1 mol e- = 96,485 C (Faraday's constant). Key concepts: balancing redox reactions (half-reaction method in acidic and basic solutions), relationship between ΔG and E° (ΔG = -nFE°), Nernst equation for non-standard conditions. Vocabulary: galvanic cell, electrolytic cell, anode, cathode, salt bridge, half-reaction, cell potential, standard reduction potential, Faraday's constant, electroplating, electrolysis, Nernst equation.

Science.teks_description: Analyze substance properties regarding intramolecular and intermolecular forces
Science.prompt: This topic covers organic chemistry functional groups and reactions for 11th grade Science students (TEKS C.7(D)). Students should understand how functional groups determine reactivity and properties. Major functional groups and their properties: alcohols (-OH, polar, hydrogen bonding, used as solvents), aldehydes (R-CHO, carbonyl at end of chain), ketones (R-CO-R, carbonyl within chain), carboxylic acids (-COOH, weak acids, found in vinegar, citrus), esters (-COO-, sweet odors, fats), amines (-NH2, basic, found in amino acids), amides (-CONH2, peptide bonds in proteins), ethers (R-O-R, relatively unreactive). Reaction types: addition (across double bonds in alkenes), substitution (one group replaces another, common in aromatics), elimination (removal of atoms to form double bond), condensation/dehydration (two molecules join, water removed), hydrolysis (water breaks bonds). Polymers: addition polymers (polyethylene, PVC) and condensation polymers (nylon, polyester, proteins). Intermolecular forces determine physical properties: hydrogen bonding raises boiling points, molecular size affects London forces. Vocabulary: functional group, alcohol, aldehyde, ketone, carboxylic acid, ester, amine, amide, addition reaction, substitution, elimination, condensation, polymer, monomer, isomer.

Science.teks_description: Analyze and critique scientific explanations using empirical evidence in physics
Science.prompt: This topic covers advanced physics for 11th grade Science students (TEKS Phy.4(A)). At this level, students deepen their understanding through rigorous mathematical analysis and real-world applications. Students should analyze physical systems using multiple representations (graphs, equations, diagrams, verbal descriptions) and evaluate the validity and limitations of physical models. Key areas: advanced mechanics (rotational dynamics, fluid mechanics), thermodynamics (laws, PV diagrams, entropy), wave phenomena (interference, diffraction, standing waves), electromagnetism (magnetic fields, induction), and introduction to modern physics (quantum and relativity concepts). Laboratory skills: designing experiments, error analysis, data linearization, connecting experimental results to theoretical predictions. Mathematical tools: trigonometry, vector analysis, calculus concepts (rates of change, areas under curves). Vocabulary: model, idealization, approximation, systematic error, linearization, dimensional analysis, vector decomposition, conservation law, symmetry, boundary condition.

Science.teks_description: Apply conservation of energy including fluid dynamics and pressure systems
Science.prompt: This topic covers fluid mechanics for 11th grade Science students (TEKS Phy.7(C)). Students should apply conservation of energy to fluid systems. Fluid properties: density ρ = m/V, pressure P = F/A (pascals). Fluid statics: hydrostatic pressure P = P0 + ρgh (increases with depth); Pascal's principle (pressure transmitted undiminished in enclosed fluid, basis for hydraulic systems F1/A1 = F2/A2). Buoyancy: Archimedes' principle, Fb = ρfluid·Vdisplaced·g; floating condition (ρobject < ρfluid). Fluid dynamics: continuity equation A1v1 = A2v2 (conservation of mass for incompressible flow); Bernoulli's equation P + ½ρv² + ρgh = constant (conservation of energy for ideal fluids). Bernoulli's principle: faster flow = lower pressure. Applications: airplane wings, carburetors, blood flow, water towers, venturi meters. Real fluids: viscosity causes drag, Reynolds number determines laminar vs. turbulent flow. Vocabulary: density, pressure, buoyancy, Archimedes' principle, Pascal's principle, continuity equation, Bernoulli's equation, viscosity, laminar, turbulent, ideal fluid, hydrostatic.

Science.teks_description: Apply conservation of energy using work-energy theorem and thermal energy transformations
Science.prompt: This topic covers thermodynamics for 11th grade Science students (TEKS Phy.7(C)). Students should apply conservation of energy to thermal systems including the laws of thermodynamics. First law: ΔU = Q - W (internal energy change = heat added - work done by system). Zeroth law: defines thermal equilibrium and temperature. Second law: entropy of isolated system never decreases; heat flows spontaneously from hot to cold; no perfect heat engine. Third law: absolute zero unattainable. Work by gas: W = PΔV (area under PV curve). Thermodynamic processes: isothermal (constant T), isobaric (constant P), isochoric (constant V), adiabatic (Q = 0). Heat engines: efficiency η = W/QH = 1 - QC/QH; Carnot efficiency ηC = 1 - TC/TH sets maximum. Specific heat: Q = mcΔT. Entropy: S = Q/T for reversible processes; ΔSuniverse ≥ 0. Key concepts: PV diagrams represent processes and cycles, connecting microscopic (molecular KE) to macroscopic (temperature, pressure). Vocabulary: internal energy, entropy, first law, second law, isothermal, adiabatic, isobaric, isochoric, heat engine, Carnot cycle, efficiency, PV diagram.

Science.teks_description: Examine simple harmonic motion and wave energy propagation in various media
Science.prompt: This topic covers oscillations and waves for 11th grade Science students (TEKS Phy.8(A)). Students should examine SHM and wave energy propagation in various media. Simple harmonic motion: restoring force proportional to displacement (F = -kx); mass-spring T = 2π√(m/k), pendulum T = 2π√(L/g). Energy in SHM: E = ½kA², continuous exchange between KE and PE. Damped oscillations: amplitude decreases over time due to friction; underdamped, critically damped, overdamped. Forced oscillations and resonance: driving frequency matching natural frequency produces maximum amplitude. Waves: transverse and longitudinal; v = fλ; speed depends on medium properties. Superposition: constructive and destructive interference. Standing waves: nodes and antinodes; strings fn = nv/(2L); open pipes fn = nv/(2L); closed pipes fn = nv/(4L) for odd n only. Energy transport: wave intensity I = P/A, decreases as 1/r² for point sources. Vocabulary: oscillation, SHM, amplitude, period, frequency, restoring force, damping, resonance, standing wave, node, antinode, harmonic, fundamental, overtone, superposition, interference.

Science.teks_description: Investigate wave behaviors: reflection, refraction, diffraction, interference, Doppler effect, polarization
Science.prompt: This topic covers optics with emphasis on wave phenomena for 11th grade Science students (TEKS Phy.8(D)). Students should investigate reflection, refraction, diffraction, interference, Doppler effect, and polarization. Reflection: law of reflection; mirrors (plane, concave, convex). Refraction: Snell's law n1sinθ1 = n2sinθ2; total internal reflection when θ > θc. Diffraction: waves spread through openings and around obstacles; most significant when aperture ≈ wavelength. Young's double-slit: d sinθ = mλ (bright fringes), demonstrates wave nature of light. Single-slit diffraction: a sinθ = mλ (dark fringes); central maximum is twice as wide as secondary maxima. Thin film interference: depends on film thickness, wavelength, and phase changes at boundaries. Diffraction gratings: sharp, well-separated maxima; d sinθ = mλ. Polarization: light wave oscillation restricted to one plane; Malus's law I = I0cos²θ; confirms transverse nature of light. Doppler effect for light: redshift (moving away) and blueshift (approaching). Vocabulary: diffraction, interference, constructive, destructive, thin film, diffraction grating, polarization, Malus's law, Doppler effect, redshift, blueshift, resolving power, coherent light.

Science.teks_description: Describe photoelectric effect and emission spectra explained by photon model
Science.prompt: This topic covers introductory quantum mechanics for 11th grade Science students (TEKS Phy.9(A)). Students should understand the photoelectric effect and emission spectra as evidence for quantized energy. Photoelectric effect: light ejects electrons from metals; observations — threshold frequency exists (no electrons below it regardless of intensity), KE of electrons depends on frequency not intensity, emission is instantaneous. Einstein's explanation: E = hf, photons carry discrete energy packets; KEmax = hf - φ (work function). Emission spectra: heated atoms emit light at specific discrete wavelengths (line spectra); each element has unique spectrum. Bohr model: electrons occupy discrete energy levels; photon emitted/absorbed when electron transitions between levels, E = hf = Ei - Ef. For hydrogen: En = -13.6/n² eV. Wave-particle duality: light shows both wave and particle behavior; de Broglie: λ = h/mv for matter waves. Heisenberg uncertainty: ΔxΔp ≥ ℏ/2. Key concepts: quantization of energy, photon model of light, atomic spectra as atomic fingerprints, quantum numbers. Vocabulary: photon, photoelectric effect, work function, threshold frequency, emission spectrum, Bohr model, energy level, wave-particle duality, de Broglie wavelength, quantized, Planck's constant.

Science.teks_description: Provide examples of nuclear phenomena applications including fission, fusion, and radiation therapy
Science.prompt: This topic covers nuclear physics for 11th grade Science students (TEKS Phy.9(D)). Students should understand nuclear processes and their applications. Nuclear structure: protons and neutrons held by strong nuclear force (short-range, overcomes electromagnetic repulsion). Radioactive decay: alpha (He-4 nucleus emission), beta-minus (n → p + e- + antineutrino), beta-plus (p → n + e+ + neutrino), gamma (high-energy photon). Half-life: N = N0(½)^(t/t½); used in dating and medical applications. Nuclear fission: heavy nucleus splits into lighter nuclei plus neutrons; chain reaction requires critical mass; controlled in reactors (control rods absorb neutrons), uncontrolled in weapons. Nuclear fusion: light nuclei combine (H → He in stars); requires extreme temperature and pressure; potential future energy source (tokamak, ITER). Mass-energy equivalence: E = mc²; binding energy from mass defect. Applications: nuclear power, medical imaging (PET, SPECT), radiation therapy, radiometric dating, nuclear medicine, smoke detectors, food irradiation. Vocabulary: fission, fusion, half-life, radioactive decay, alpha, beta, gamma, strong nuclear force, chain reaction, critical mass, binding energy, mass defect, radiation therapy.

Science.teks_description: Explain superposition of quantum states and relativistic concepts related to wave-particle duality
Science.prompt: This topic covers special relativity for 11th grade Science students (TEKS Phy.9(C)). Students should understand Einstein's postulates and key consequences. Two postulates: (1) laws of physics are the same in all inertial reference frames, (2) speed of light c is constant for all observers. Consequences: time dilation (Δt = γΔt0, moving clocks run slower), length contraction (L = L0/γ, moving objects shorter in direction of motion), relativity of simultaneity. Lorentz factor: γ = 1/√(1 - v²/c²); approaches 1 at low speeds, approaches infinity as v approaches c. Mass-energy equivalence: E = mc² (rest energy); total energy E = γmc²; relativistic momentum p = γmv. Nothing with mass can reach c (would require infinite energy). Evidence: muon decay experiments (extended lifetime due to time dilation), particle accelerator results (mass increase at high speeds), GPS satellite corrections, atomic clock experiments. Connections to quantum physics: wave-particle duality, attempts at quantum gravity unification. Vocabulary: special relativity, postulate, time dilation, length contraction, Lorentz factor, proper time, proper length, rest mass, relativistic momentum, spacetime, inertial reference frame, simultaneity.
